question:
Prove that there exists a constant $c>0$ such that in every
nontrivial finite group $G$ there exists a sequence of length
at most $c \log |G|$ with the property that each element of $G$
equals the product of some subsequence. (The elements of $G$ in the
sequence are not required to be distinct. A \emph{subsequence}
of a sequence is obtained by selecting some of the terms,
not necessarily consecutive, without reordering them; for
example, $4, 4, 2$ is a subsequence of $2, 4, 6, 4, 2$, but
$2, 2, 4$ is not.)
solution:
For notational convenience, we will interpret the problem as
allowing the empty subsequence, whose product is the identity element of
the group. To solve the problem in the interpretation where the empty
subsequence is not allowed, simply append the identity element to the sequence
given by one of the following solutions.

\textbf{First solution:}
Put $n = |G|$.
We will say that a sequence $S$ \emph{produces}
an element $g \in G$ if $g$ occurs as the product of some subsequence
of $S$.
Let $H$ be the set of elements produced by the sequence $S$.

Start with $S$ equal to the empty sequence. If at any point
the set $H^{-1}H = \{h_1 h_2: h_1^{-1}, h_2 \in H\}$ fails to be
all of $G$, extend $S$ by appending an element $g$ of $G$ not in
$H^{-1} H$. Then $Hg \cap H$ must be empty, otherwise there would
be an equation of the form $h_1 g= h_2 $ with $h_1, h_2 \in G$,
or $g = h_1^{-1} h_2$, a contradiction. Thus we can extend $S$ by one
element and double the size of $H$.

After $k \leq \log_2 n$ steps, we must obtain a sequence $S
= a_1,\dots,a_k$ for which $H^{-1} H = G$. Then
the sequence $a_k^{-1}, \dots, a_1^{-1}, a_1, \dots, a_k$
produces all of $G$ and has length at most $(2/\ln 2) \ln n$.

\textbf{Second solution:}

Put $m = |H|$. We will show that we can append one element
$g$ to $S$ so that the resulting sequence of $k+1$ elements will produce
at least $2m-m^2/n$ elements of $G$. To see this, we compute
\begin{align*}
\sum_{g \in G} |H \cup Hg|
&= \sum_{g \in G} (|H| + |Hg| - |H \cap Hg|) \\
&= 2mn - \sum_{g \in G} |H \cap Hg| \\
&= 2mn - |\{(g,h) \in G^2: h \in H \cap Hg\}| \\
&= 2mn - \sum_{h \in H} |\{g \in G: h \in Hg\}| \\
&= 2mn - \sum_{h \in H} |H^{-1} h| \\
&= 2mn - m^2.
\end{align*}
By the pigeonhole principle, we have $|H \cup Hg| \geq 2m - m^2/n$ for
some choice of $g$, as claimed.

In other words, by extending the sequence by one element,
we can replace the ratio $s = 1-m/n$ (i.e., the fraction
of elements of $G$ not generated by $S$)
by a quantity no greater than
\[
1-(2m-m^2/n)/n = s^2.
\]
We start out with $k = 0$ and $s = 1 - 1/n$;
after $k$ steps, we have $s \leq (1-1/n)^{2^k}$.
It is enough to prove that for some $c > 0$, we can always find
an integer $k \leq c \ln n$ such that
\[
\left(1 - \frac{1}{n} \right)^{2^k} < \frac{1}{n},
\]
as then we have $n-m < 1$ and hence $H = G$.

To obtain this last inequality, put
\[
k = \lfloor 2 \log_2 n \rfloor < (2/\ln 2) \ln n,
\]
so that $2^{k+1} \geq n^2$.
From the facts that $\ln n \leq \ln 2 + (n-2)/2 \leq n/2$ and
$\ln (1-1/n) < -1/n$ for all $n \geq 2$, we have
\[
2^k \ln \left( 1 - \frac{1}{n} \right) < -\frac{n^2}{2n} =  -\frac{n}{2} < -\ln n,
\]
yielding the desired inequality.

\textbf{Remark:} An alternate approach in the second solution
is to distinguish betwen the cases of $H$ small (i.e.,
$m < n^{1/2}$, in which case $m$ can be replaced by a value
no less than $2m-1$) and $H$ large.
This strategy is used in a number of recent results
of Bourgain, Tao, Helfgott, and others on \emph{small doubling}
or \emph{small tripling}
of subsets of finite groups.

In the second solution, if we avoid the rather weak inequality
$\ln n \leq n/2$, we instead get sequences of length
$\log_2 (n \ln n) = \log_2(n) + \log_2 (\ln n)$.
This is close to optimal: one cannot use fewer than $\log_2 n$
terms because the number of subsequences must be at least $n$.

descriptive_long.question:
Prove that there exists a constant $positvconst>0$ such that in every
nontrivial finite group $groupglo$ there exists a sequence of length
at most $positvconst \log |groupglo|$ with the property that each element of $groupglo$
equals the product of some subsequence. (The elements of $groupglo$ in the
sequence are not required to be distinct. A \emph{subsequence}
of a sequence is obtained by selecting some of the terms,
not necessarily consecutive, without reordering them; for
example, $4, 4, 2$ is a subsequence of $2, 4, 6, 4, 2$, but
$2, 2, 4$ is not.)
descriptive_long.solution:
For notational convenience, we will interpret the problem as
allowing the empty subsequence, whose product is the identity element of
the group. To solve the problem in the interpretation where the empty
subsequence is not allowed, simply append the identity element to the sequence
given by one of the following solutions.

\textbf{First solution:}
Put $grouplsz = |groupglo|$.
We will say that a sequence $seqwhole$ \emph{produces}
an element $elemvar \in groupglo$ if $elemvar$ occurs as the product of some subsequence
of $seqwhole$.
Let $subgenset$ be the set of elements produced by the sequence $seqwhole$.

Start with $seqwhole$ equal to the empty sequence. If at any point
the set $subgenset^{-1}subgenset = \{\elemone \elemtwo: \elemone^{-1}, \elemtwo \in subgenset\}$ fails to be
all of $groupglo$, extend $seqwhole$ by appending an element $elemvar$ of $groupglo$ not in
$subgenset^{-1} subgenset$. Then $subgenset elemvar \cap subgenset$ must be empty, otherwise there would
be an equation of the form $\elemone elemvar= \elemtwo $ with $\elemone, \elemtwo \in groupglo$,
or $elemvar = \elemone^{-1} \elemtwo$, a contradiction. Thus we can extend $seqwhole$ by one
element and double the size of $subgenset$.

After $stepcount \leq \log_2 grouplsz$ steps, we must obtain a sequence $seqwhole
= singone,\dots,singlast$ for which $subgenset^{-1} subgenset = groupglo$. Then
the sequence $singlast^{-1}, \dots, singone^{-1}, singone, \dots, singlast$
produces all of $groupglo$ and has length at most $(2/\ln 2) \ln grouplsz$.

\textbf{Second solution:}

Put $producedct = |subgenset|$. We will show that we can append one element
$elemvar$ to $seqwhole$ so that the resulting sequence of $stepcount+1$ elements will produce
at least $2producedct-producedct^2/grouplsz$ elements of $groupglo$. To see this, we compute
\begin{align*}
\sum_{elemvar \in groupglo} |subgenset \cup subgenset elemvar|
&= \sum_{elemvar \in groupglo} (|subgenset| + |subgenset elemvar| - |subgenset \cap subgenset elemvar|) \\
&= 2producedct\,grouplsz - \sum_{elemvar \in groupglo} |subgenset \cap subgenset elemvar| \\
&= 2producedct\,grouplsz - |\{(elemvar,\elemalt) \in groupglo^2: \elemalt \in subgenset \cap subgenset elemvar\}| \\
&= 2producedct\,grouplsz - \sum_{\elemalt \in subgenset} |\{elemvar \in groupglo: \elemalt \in subgenset elemvar\}| \\
&= 2producedct\,grouplsz - \sum_{\elemalt \in subgenset} |subgenset^{-1} \elemalt| \\
&= 2producedct\,grouplsz - producedct^2.
\end{align*}
By the pigeonhole principle, we have $|subgenset \cup subgenset elemvar| \geq 2producedct - producedct^2/grouplsz$ for
some choice of $elemvar$, as claimed.

In other words, by extending the sequence by one element,
we can replace the ratio $ratioleft = 1-producedct/grouplsz$ (i.e., the fraction
of elements of $groupglo$ not generated by $seqwhole$)
by a quantity no greater than
\[
1-\frac{2producedct-producedct^2/grouplsz}{grouplsz} = ratioleft^2.
\]
We start out with $stepcount = 0$ and $ratioleft = 1 - 1/grouplsz$;
after $stepcount$ steps, we have $ratioleft \leq (1-1/grouplsz)^{2^{stepcount}}$.
It is enough to prove that for some $positvconst > 0$, we can always find
an integer $stepcount \leq positvconst \ln grouplsz$ such that
\[
\left(1 - \frac{1}{grouplsz} \right)^{2^{stepcount}} < \frac{1}{grouplsz},
\]
as then we have $grouplsz-producedct < 1$ and hence $subgenset = groupglo$.

To obtain this last inequality, put
\[
stepcount = \lfloor 2 \log_2 grouplsz \rfloor < (2/\ln 2) \ln grouplsz,
\]
so that $2^{stepcount+1} \geq grouplsz^2$.
From the facts that $\ln grouplsz \leq \ln 2 + (grouplsz-2)/2 \leq grouplsz/2$ and
$\ln (1-1/grouplsz) < -1/grouplsz$ for all $grouplsz \geq 2$, we have
\[
2^{stepcount} \ln \left( 1 - \frac{1}{grouplsz} \right) < -\frac{grouplsz^2}{2grouplsz} =  -\frac{grouplsz}{2} < -\ln grouplsz,
\]
yielding the desired inequality.

\textbf{Remark:} An alternate approach in the second solution
is to distinguish betwen the cases of $subgenset$ small (i.e.,
$producedct < grouplsz^{1/2}$, in which case $producedct$ can be replaced by a value
no less than $2producedct-1$) and $subgenset$ large.
This strategy is used in a number of recent results
of Bourgain, Tao, Helfgott, and others on \emph{small doubling}
or \emph{small tripling}
of subsets of finite groups.

In the second solution, if we avoid the rather weak inequality
$\ln grouplsz \leq grouplsz/2$, we instead get sequences of length
$\log_2 (grouplsz \ln grouplsz) = \log_2(grouplsz) + \log_2 (\ln grouplsz)$.
This is close to optimal: one cannot use fewer than $\log_2 grouplsz$
terms because the number of subsequences must be at least $grouplsz$.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Prove that there exists a constant $pinecone>0$ such that in every
nontrivial finite group $riverboat$ there exists a sequence of length
at most $pinecone \log |riverboat|$ with the property that each element of $riverboat$
equals the product of some subsequence. (The elements of $riverboat$ in the
sequence are not required to be distinct. A \emph{subsequence}
of a sequence is obtained by selecting some of the terms,
not necessarily consecutive, without reordering them; for
example, $4, 4, 2$ is a subsequence of $2, 4, 6, 4, 2$, but
$2, 2, 4$ is not.)
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
For notational convenience, we will interpret the problem as
allowing the empty subsequence, whose product is the identity element of
the group. To solve the problem in the interpretation where the empty
subsequence is not allowed, simply append the identity element to the sequence
given by one of the following solutions.

\textbf{First solution:}
Put $gemstone = |riverboat|$.
We will say that a sequence $honeycomb$ \emph{produces}
an element $hummingbird \in riverboat$ if $hummingbird$ occurs as the product of some subsequence
of $honeycomb$.
Let $clifftop$ be the set of elements produced by the sequence $honeycomb$.

Start with $honeycomb$ equal to the empty sequence. If at any point
the set $clifftop^{-1}clifftop = \{crosswind driftwood: crosswind^{-1}, driftwood \in clifftop\}$ fails to be
all of $riverboat$, extend $honeycomb$ by appending an element $hummingbird$ of $riverboat$ not in
$clifftop^{-1} clifftop$. Then $clifftophummingbird \cap clifftop$ must be empty, otherwise there would
be an equation of the form $crosswind hummingbird= driftwood$ with $crosswind, driftwood \in riverboat$,
or $hummingbird = crosswind^{-1} driftwood$, a contradiction. Thus we can extend $honeycomb$ by one
element and double the size of $clifftop$.

After $starlight \leq \log_2 gemstone$ steps, we must obtain a sequence $honeycomb
= moonlight,\dots,wildfire$ for which $clifftop^{-1} clifftop = riverboat$. Then
the sequence $wildfire^{-1}, \dots, moonlight^{-1}, moonlight, \dots, wildfire$
produces all of $riverboat$ and has length at most $(2/\ln 2) \ln gemstone$.

\textbf{Second solution:}

Put $afterglow = |clifftop|$. We will show that we can append one element
$hummingbird$ to $honeycomb$ so that the resulting sequence of $starlight+1$ elements will produce
at least $2afterglow-afterglow^2/gemstone$ elements of $riverboat$. To see this, we compute
\begin{align*}
\sum_{hummingbird \in riverboat} |clifftop \cup clifftophummingbird|
&= \sum_{hummingbird \in riverboat} (|clifftop| + |clifftophummingbird| - |clifftop \cap clifftophummingbird|) \\
&= 2afterglow gemstone - \sum_{hummingbird \in riverboat} |clifftop \cap clifftophummingbird| \\
&= 2afterglow gemstone - |\{(hummingbird,snowflake) \in riverboat^2: snowflake \in clifftop \cap clifftophummingbird\}| \\
&= 2afterglow gemstone - \sum_{snowflake \in clifftop} |\{hummingbird \in riverboat: snowflake \in clifftophummingbird\}| \\
&= 2afterglow gemstone - \sum_{snowflake \in clifftop} |clifftop^{-1} snowflake| \\
&= 2afterglow gemstone - afterglow^2.
\end{align*}
By the pigeonhole principle, we have $|clifftop \cup clifftophummingbird| \geq 2afterglow - afterglow^2/gemstone$ for
some choice of $hummingbird$, as claimed.

In other words, by extending the sequence by one element,
we can replace the ratio $woodpecker = 1-afterglow/gemstone$ (i.e., the fraction
of elements of $riverboat$ not generated by $honeycomb$)
by a quantity no greater than
\[
1-(2afterglow-afterglow^2/gemstone)/gemstone = woodpecker^2.
\]
We start out with $starlight = 0$ and $woodpecker = 1 - 1/gemstone$;
after $starlight$ steps, we have $woodpecker \leq (1-1/gemstone)^{2^{starlight}}$.
It is enough to prove that for some $pinecone > 0$, we can always find
an integer $starlight \leq pinecone \ln gemstone$ such that
\[
\left(1 - \frac{1}{gemstone} \right)^{2^{starlight}} < \frac{1}{gemstone},
\]
as then we have $gemstone-afterglow < 1$ and hence $clifftop = riverboat$.

To obtain this last inequality, put
\[
starlight = \lfloor 2 \log_2 gemstone \rfloor < (2/\ln 2) \ln gemstone,
\]
so that $2^{starlight+1} \geq gemstone^2$.
From the facts that $\ln gemstone \leq \ln 2 + (gemstone-2)/2 \leq gemstone/2$ and
$\ln (1-1/gemstone) < -1/gemstone$ for all $gemstone \geq 2$, we have
\[
2^{starlight} \ln \left( 1 - \frac{1}{gemstone} \right) < -\frac{gemstone^2}{2gemstone} =  -\frac{gemstone}{2} < -\ln gemstone,
\]
yielding the desired inequality.

\textbf{Remark:} An alternate approach in the second solution
is to distinguish betwen the cases of $clifftop$ small (i.e.,
$afterglow < gemstone^{1/2}$, in which case $afterglow$ can be replaced by a value
no less than $2afterglow-1$) and $clifftop$ large.
This strategy is used in a number of recent results
of Bourgain, Tao, Helfgott, and others on \emph{small doubling}
or \emph{small tripling}
of subsets of finite groups.

In the second solution, if we avoid the rather weak inequality
$\ln gemstone \leq gemstone/2$, we instead get sequences of length
$\log_2 (gemstone \ln gemstone) = \log_2(gemstone) + \log_2 (\ln gemstone)$.
This is close to optimal: one cannot use fewer than $\log_2 gemstone$
terms because the number of subsequences must be at least $gemstone$.}

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Prove that there exists a constant $variable>0$ such that in every
nontrivial finite group $ungrouped$ there exists a sequence of length
at most $variable \log |ungrouped|$ with the property that each element of $ungrouped$
equals the product of some subsequence. (The elements of $ungrouped$ in the
sequence are not required to be distinct. A \emph{subsequence}
of a sequence is obtained by selecting some of the terms,
not necessarily consecutive, without reordering them; for
example, $4, 4, 2$ is a subsequence of $2, 4, 6, 4, 2$, but
$2, 2, 4$ is not.)
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
For notational convenience, we will interpret the problem as
allowing the empty subsequence, whose product is the identity element of
the group. To solve the problem in the interpretation where the empty
subsequence is not allowed, simply append the identity element to the sequence
given by one of the following solutions.

\textbf{First solution:}
Put $minisize = |ungrouped|$.
We will say that a sequence $singleton$ \emph{produces}
an element $insideone \in ungrouped$ if $insideone$ occurs as the product of some subsequence
of $singleton$.
Let $universal$ be the set of elements produced by the sequence $singleton$.

Start with $singleton$ equal to the empty sequence. If at any point
the set $universal^{-1}universal = \{outsiderone outsidertwo: outsiderone^{-1}, outsidertwo \in universal\}$ fails to be
all of $ungrouped$, extend $singleton$ by appending an element $insideone$ of $ungrouped$ not in
$universal^{-1} universal$. Then $universal insideone \cap universal$ must be empty, otherwise there would
be an equation of the form $outsiderone insideone= outsidertwo $ with $outsiderone, outsidertwo \in ungrouped$,
or $insideone = outsiderone^{-1} outsidertwo$, a contradiction. Thus we can extend $singleton$ by one
element and double the size of $universal$.

After $endlessn \leq \log_2 minisize$ steps, we must obtain a sequence $singleton
= compoundone,\dots,compoundend$ for which $universal^{-1} universal = ungrouped$. Then
the sequence $compoundend^{-1}, \dots, compoundone^{-1}, compoundone, \dots, compoundend$
produces all of $ungrouped$ and has length at most $(2/\ln 2) \ln minisize$.

\textbf{Second solution:}

Put $emptiness = |universal|$. We will show that we can append one element
$insideone$ to $singleton$ so that the resulting sequence of $endlessn+1$ elements will produce
at least $2 emptiness minisize-emptiness^2/minisize$ elements of $ungrouped$. To see this, we compute
\begin{align*}
\sum_{insideone \in ungrouped} |universal \cup universal insideone|
&= \sum_{insideone \in ungrouped} (|universal| + |universal insideone| - |universal \cap universal insideone|) \\
&= 2 emptiness minisize - \sum_{insideone \in ungrouped} |universal \cap universal insideone| \\
&= 2 emptiness minisize - |\{(insideone,outsider) \in ungrouped^2: outsider \in universal \cap universal insideone\}| \\
&= 2 emptiness minisize - \sum_{outsider \in universal} |\{insideone \in ungrouped: outsider \in universal insideone\}| \\
&= 2 emptiness minisize - \sum_{outsider \in universal} |universal^{-1} outsider| \\
&= 2 emptiness minisize - emptiness^2.
\end{align*}
By the pigeonhole principle, we have $|universal \cup universal insideone| \geq 2emptiness - emptiness^2/minisize$ for
some choice of $insideone$, as claimed.

In other words, by extending the sequence by one element,
we can replace the ratio $plentiful = 1-emptiness/minisize$ (i.e., the fraction
of elements of $ungrouped$ not generated by $singleton$)
by a quantity no greater than
\[
1-(2emptiness-emptiness^2/minisize)/minisize = plentiful^2.
\]
We start out with $endlessn = 0$ and $plentiful = 1 - 1/minisize$;
after $endlessn$ steps, we have $plentiful \leq (1-1/minisize)^{2^{endlessn}}$.
It is enough to prove that for some $variable > 0$, we can always find
an integer $endlessn \leq variable \ln minisize$ such that
\[
\left(1 - \frac{1}{minisize} \right)^{2^{endlessn}} < \frac{1}{minisize},
\]
as then we have $minisize-emptiness < 1$ and hence $universal = ungrouped$.

To obtain this last inequality, put
\[
endlessn = \lfloor 2 \log_2 minisize \rfloor < (2/\ln 2) \ln minisize,
\]
so that $2^{endlessn+1} \geq minisize^2$.
From the facts that $\ln minisize \leq \ln 2 + (minisize-2)/2 \leq minisize/2$ and
$\ln (1-1/minisize) < -1/minisize$ for all $minisize \geq 2$, we have
\[
2^{endlessn} \ln \left( 1 - \frac{1}{minisize} \right) < -\frac{minisize^2}{2minisize} =  -\frac{minisize}{2} < -\ln minisize,
\]
yielding the desired inequality.

\textbf{Remark:} An alternate approach in the second solution
is to distinguish betwen the cases of $universal$ small (i.e.,
$emptiness < minisize^{1/2}$, in which case $emptiness$ can be replaced by a value
no less than $2emptiness-1$) and $universal$ large.
This strategy is used in a number of recent results
of Bourgain, Tao, Helfgott, and others on \emph{small doubling}
or \emph{small tripling}
of subsets of finite groups.

In the second solution, if we avoid the rather weak inequality
$\ln minisize \leq minisize/2$, we instead get sequences of length
$\log_2 (minisize \ln minisize) = \log_2(minisize) + \log_2 (\ln minisize)$.
This is close to optimal: one cannot use fewer than $\log_2 minisize$
terms because the number of subsequences must be at least $minisize$.  }

garbled_string.question:
Prove that there exists a constant $jhvlktse>0$ such that in every
nontrivial finite group $qzxwvtnp$ there exists a sequence of length
at most $jhvlktse \log |qzxwvtnp|$ with the property that each element of $qzxwvtnp$
equals the product of some subsequence. (The elements of $qzxwvtnp$ in the
sequence are not required to be distinct. A \emph{subsequence}
of a sequence is obtained by selecting some of the terms,
not necessarily consecutive, without reordering them; for
example, $4, 4, 2$ is a subsequence of $2, 4, 6, 4, 2$, but
$2, 2, 4$ is not.)
garbled_string.solution:
For notational convenience, we will interpret the problem as
allowing the empty subsequence, whose product is the identity element of
the group. To solve the problem in the interpretation where the empty
subsequence is not allowed, simply append the identity element to the sequence
given by one of the following solutions.

\textbf{First solution:}
Put $gdjoukhr = |qzxwvtnp|$.
We will say that a sequence $fmvqslre$ \emph{produces}
an element $wjslmpqe \in qzxwvtnp$ if $wjslmpqe$ occurs as the product of some subsequence
of $fmvqslre$.
Let $hjgrksla$ be the set of elements produced by the sequence $fmvqslre$.

Start with $fmvqslre$ equal to the empty sequence. If at any point
the set $hjgrksla^{-1}hjgrksla = \{sdzmbpqa lxvhzqwe: sdzmbpqa^{-1}, lxvhzqwe \in hjgrksla\}$ fails to be
all of $qzxwvtnp$, extend $fmvqslre$ by appending an element $wjslmpqe$ of $qzxwvtnp$ not in
$hjgrksla^{-1} hjgrksla$. Then $hjgrksla wjslmpqe \cap hjgrksla$ must be empty, otherwise there would
be an equation of the form $sdzmbpqa wjslmpqe= lxvhzqwe $ with $sdzmbpqa, lxvhzqwe \in qzxwvtnp$,
or $wjslmpqe = sdzmbpqa^{-1} lxvhzqwe$, a contradiction. Thus we can extend $fmvqslre$ by one
element and double the size of $hjgrksla$.

After $tbvysoam \leq \log_2 gdjoukhr$ steps, we must obtain a sequence $fmvqslre
= yczstigb,\dots,kngwyhza$ for which $hjgrksla^{-1} hjgrksla = qzxwvtnp$. Then
the sequence $kngwyhza^{-1}, \dots, yczstigb^{-1}, yczstigb, \dots, kngwyhza$
produces all of $qzxwvtnp$ and has length at most $(2/\ln 2) \ln gdjoukhr$.

\textbf{Second solution:}

Put $pznrxvlu = |hjgrksla|$. We will show that we can append one element
$wjslmpqe$ to $fmvqslre$ so that the resulting sequence of $tbvysoam+1$ elements will produce
at least $2pznrxvlu-pznrxvlu^2/gdjoukhr$ elements of $qzxwvtnp$. To see this, we compute
\begin{align*}
\sum_{wjslmpqe \in qzxwvtnp} |hjgrksla \cup hjgrksla wjslmpqe|
&= \sum_{wjslmpqe \in qzxwvtnp} (|hjgrksla| + |hjgrksla wjslmpqe| - |hjgrksla \cap hjgrksla wjslmpqe|) \\
&= 2pznrxvlu gdjoukhr - \sum_{wjslmpqe \in qzxwvtnp} |hjgrksla \cap hjgrksla wjslmpqe| \\
&= 2pznrxvlu gdjoukhr - |\{(wjslmpqe,rqltvnso) \in qzxwvtnp^2: rqltvnso \in hjgrksla \cap hjgrksla wjslmpqe\}| \\
&= 2pznrxvlu gdjoukhr - \sum_{rqltvnso \in hjgrksla} |\{wjslmpqe \in qzxwvtnp: rqltvnso \in hjgrksla wjslmpqe\}| \\
&= 2pznrxvlu gdjoukhr - \sum_{rqltvnso \in hjgrksla} |hjgrksla^{-1} rqltvnso| \\
&= 2pznrxvlu gdjoukhr - pznrxvlu^2.
\end{align*}
By the pigeonhole principle, we have $|hjgrksla \cup hjgrksla wjslmpqe| \geq 2pznrxvlu - pznrxvlu^2/gdjoukhr$ for
some choice of $wjslmpqe$, as claimed.

In other words, by extending the sequence by one element,
we can replace the ratio $vnrzqtla = 1-pznrxvlu/gdjoukhr$ (i.e., the fraction
of elements of $qzxwvtnp$ not generated by $fmvqslre$)
by a quantity no greater than
\[
1-(2pznrxvlu-pznrxvlu^2/gdjoukhr)/gdjoukhr = vnrzqtla^2.
\]
We start out with $tbvysoam = 0$ and $vnrzqtla = 1 - 1/gdjoukhr$;
after $tbvysoam$ steps, we have $vnrzqtla \leq (1-1/gdjoukhr)^{2^{tbvysoam}}$.
It is enough to prove that for some $jhvlktse > 0$, we can always find
an integer $tbvysoam \leq jhvlktse \ln gdjoukhr$ such that
\[
\left(1 - \frac{1}{gdjoukhr} \right)^{2^{tbvysoam}} < \frac{1}{gdjoukhr},
\]
as then we have $gdjoukhr-pznrxvlu < 1$ and hence $hjgrksla = qzxwvtnp$.

To obtain this last inequality, put
\[
tbvysoam = \lfloor 2 \log_2 gdjoukhr \rfloor < (2/\ln 2) \ln gdjoukhr,
\]
so that $2^{tbvysoam+1} \geq gdjoukhr^2$.
From the facts that $\ln gdjoukhr \leq \ln 2 + (gdjoukhr-2)/2 \leq gdjoukhr/2$ and
$\ln (1-1/gdjoukhr) < -1/gdjoukhr$ for all $gdjoukhr \geq 2$, we have
\[
2^{tbvysoam} \ln \left( 1 - \frac{1}{gdjoukhr} \right) < -\frac{gdjoukhr^2}{2gdjoukhr} =  -\frac{gdjoukhr}{2} < -\ln gdjoukhr,
\]
yielding the desired inequality.

\textbf{Remark:} An alternate approach in the second solution
is to distinguish betwen the cases of $hjgrksla$ small (i.e.,
$pznrxvlu < gdjoukhr^{1/2}$, in which case $pznrxvlu$ can be replaced by a value
no less than $2pznrxvlu-1$) and $hjgrksla$ large.
This strategy is used in a number of recent results
of Bourgain, Tao, Helfgott, and others on \emph{small doubling}
or \emph{small tripling}
of subsets of finite groups.

In the second solution, if we avoid the rather weak inequality
$\ln gdjoukhr \leq gdjoukhr/2$, we instead get sequences of length
$\log_2 (gdjoukhr \ln gdjoukhr) = \log_2(gdjoukhr) + \log_2 (\ln gdjoukhr)$.
This is close to optimal: one cannot use fewer than $\log_2 gdjoukhr$
terms because the number of subsequences must be at least $gdjoukhr$.}

kernel_variant.question:
Let n \geq  2 and let G be a finite group of order n.  Show that there is an absolute constant c = 7 for which one can find elements a_1, \ldots  , a_k of G with
	k \leq  c \cdot  log_3 n
so that the sequence
	( 1 , a_1 , a_2 , \ldots  , a_k , a_k^{-1} , a_{k-1}^{-1} , \ldots  , a_1^{-1} )
has the following property: every element of G can be written as the ordered product of the terms of some non-empty subsequence of that sequence (a subsequence is obtained by deleting any of the displayed terms, without changing the order of the remaining terms).

(The constant 7 is not best possible; it is chosen only to keep the numerical statement simple.)
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we write log for the logarithm to base 3.  The argument has two parts.


1.  Constructing a rapidly growing set H.


Say that a sequence S of elements of G *produces* g \in  G if g is the product of some subsequence of S.  Denote
	H(S) = { g \in  G : g is produced by S }.

Start with the one-term sequence S_0 = (1); then H_0 = {1}.  Suppose after some steps we have a sequence S whose produced set is H = H(S), and assume that H^{-1}H \neq  G.  Choose an element
	g \notin  H^{-1}H
and append it to S, obtaining the longer sequence S'.  Every element obtained with the new final term is of the form h g with h \in  H, so H(S') = H \cup  H g.

Claim:  H g \cap  H = \emptyset .  Indeed, were h_1 g = h_2 with h_1,h_2 \in  H, then g = h_1^{-1}h_2 \in  H^{-1}H, contradicting the choice of g.  Consequently
	|H(S')| = |H| + |H g| = 2 |H|,
so each extension *doubles* the size of H.

After k steps we therefore have |H| = 2^k.  We must stop when no element lies outside H^{-1}H, i.e. when H^{-1}H = G.  Since |H| \leq  n at every stage, doubling can occur at most \lceil log_2 n\rceil  steps, so we obtain a sequence
	S_k = (a_1, \ldots  , a_k)          with k \leq  \lceil log_2 n\rceil  \leq  2 log n,
(in base-3 units this is k \leq  (log_23) log n < 1.6 log n).
For this S_k we have, by construction,
	H_k^{-1} H_k = G  where H_k = H(S_k).


2.  A short sequence producing the whole group.


Form
	L = ( 1 , a_1 , \ldots  , a_k , a_k^{-1} , \ldots  , a_1^{-1} ).
Its length is
	\ell  = 1 + 2k \leq  1 + 2 log_2 n < 1 + 3.2 log n < 7 log n,
so the numerical bound (with base-3 logarithms) is satisfied.

It remains to show that every g \in  G is produced by L.
Because H_k^{-1} H_k = G, for each g there exist h_1 , h_2 \in  H_k with
	g = h_1 h_2^{-1}.
Choose subsequences of (a_1,\ldots ,a_k) whose products are h_1 and h_2; write the index sets
	I = { i_1 < \ldots  < i_r },      J = { j_1 < \ldots  < j_s }.
Then
	h_1 = a_{i_1} \ldots  a_{i_r},
	h_2 = a_{j_1} \ldots  a_{j_s}.

Inside L the inverses appear in *reverse* order,
	a_k^{-1} , \ldots  , a_1^{-1} ,
so the subsequence
	a_{i_1} \ldots  a_{i_r}   a_{j_s}^{-1} \ldots  a_{j_1}^{-1}
occurs in L (all direct a's come first, all chosen inverses come later) and multiplies to h_1 h_2^{-1} = g.  Thus every element of G is the product of some subsequence of L.


3.  Conclusion.


We have exhibited a sequence of length at most 7\cdot log_3|G| that begins with the identity and whose non-empty subsequences produce every element of the group.  Hence the desired constant works.

core_steps: Let H be the set of products obtainable from the current sequence S.
If H^{-1}H ≠ G, choose g ∉ H^{-1}H; this guarantees Hg ∩ H = ∅.
Appending g therefore expands |H| to at least 2|H| (size ≥ double).
After ≤ ⌈log₂|G|⌉ such extensions we reach H^{-1}H = G.
Append the inverses (a_k^{-1},…,a_1^{-1}) to S = (a_1,…,a_k); the resulting sequence of length ≤ (2/ln2)·ln|G| produces every element of G.

slot1.description: Choice of set used to detect ‘missing’ elements (H^{-1}H can be replaced by HH^{-1}, HH, etc., any subset closed under taking quotients that still equals G when H is sufficiently large).

slot2.description: Exact growth guarantee; the argument only needs a constant factor >1, not necessarily a full doubling.

slot3.description: Base of the logarithm used to count steps; changing the base merely rescales the constant c.

slot4.description: Precise concatenation that turns the ‘generating’ sequence into one whose subsequences produce all of G; any ordering that lists each a_i with its inverse works.

slot5.description: Numerical upper bound on length: any constant ≥ 2/ln2 suffices for c.

slot6.description: Treatment of the identity element (allowing the empty subsequence vs. appending 1_G at the end).
slot6.original: Allow empty subsequence or add identity as final term
